questions.prompt: What are the four elements of a complete close-out communication?
choices: What happened, what was done, what the client should do.

choices.label: Apology, summary, recommendation, signature.
choices.rationale: Apologising is a named common mistake from the notification-writing lesson.

choices.label: Subject line, greeting, body, sign-off.
choices.rationale: Email anatomy, not the lesson's framework. The four elements are about content.

choices.label: Severity, timeline, evidence, recommendation.
choices.rationale: Those are Incident Report sections, not close-out elements.

questions.explanation: (if anything), what was learned (when there is a real lesson). Four elements every time: what happened, what was done, what the client should do, what was learned.

questions.prompt: "What the client should do" is the element new techs most often miss. Why does it matter even when the answer is "nothing"?
choices: Naming the next step (even "none") prevents the customer from wondering what they are missing.

choices.label: Huntress requires a customer-action line in every close-out.
choices.rationale: No such requirement.

choices.label: The customer's IT manager has to sign off on the close-out.
choices.rationale: Close-outs are notifications, not approvals.

choices.label: Skipping it triggers an automatic follow-up email.
choices.rationale: Not a real platform behaviour.

questions.explanation: "No action required from your side" closes the loop. "We resolved everything" leaves the customer unsure. "No action required from your side" says the same thing and closes the loop.

questions.prompt: When should you include the "what was learned" element?

choices.label: Always, even if you have to invent one.
choices.rationale: Manufacturing a lesson learned when there is not one is a named common mistake.

choices: When there is a real one-line takeaway for the customer or for your MSP.

choices.label: Only on Critical incidents.
choices.rationale: Severity is not the criterion. Whether there is a real takeaway is.

choices.label: Only when the senior writes the close-out.
choices.rationale: Senior involvement is separate. The element rule is the same regardless.

questions.explanation: Skip when there is no useful lesson. Skip elements that do not fit. Forcing one creates "lessons" that are not.

questions.prompt: Which set of tone properties does the lesson name for close-out communications?

choices.label: Formal, hedged, apologetic.
choices.rationale: Apologetic is the notification-writing lesson's common mistake. Hedged is the vague-version failure.

choices.label: Maximally technical for full transparency.
choices.rationale: "Specific without overwhelming" is the discipline. Deep technical detail waits for the deep-dive.

choices.label: Performative and self-congratulatory to reinforce value.
choices.rationale: "Using the close-out as a sales surface" is a named common mistake.

questions.explanation: Specific without overwhelming; customer-respecting. Three tone properties: confident without triumphalism, specific without overwhelming, customer-respecting.

questions.prompt: Ending a close-out with "This is why our service is essential to your business" is a good way to reinforce the MSP's value.
questions.explanation: Named common mistake. That framing turns the close-out into marketing. Customers notice and disengage.

questions.prompt: The lesson describes "the bar" for a close-out as which property?
choices: Reads cleanly six months later when it surfaces in an audit.

choices.label: Generated automatically by the portal without tech intervention.
choices.rationale: Close-outs are written, not generated.

choices.label: Approved by Huntress support before sending.
choices.rationale: Not in the lesson.

choices.label: Sent within 10 minutes of incident resolution.
choices.rationale: Timing is not the bar. Quality is.

questions.explanation: The close-out is the durable record. It has to age well.

questions.prompt: High EDR incident, scheduled task with encoded PowerShell, standard workflow run cleanly, no re-detection. Which close-out works for the IT manager?

choices.label: "We caught and contained an EDR incident on the machine today. All resolved. Let me know if you have questions."
choices.rationale: Vague. No specifics, no actions named, no "what next." Does not close the loop.

choices: Brief close-out with timestamps and named actions.

choices.label: Same as option B but adds "you're lucky we caught this in time."
choices.rationale: Triumphalism. Positions the MSP as rescuer rather than as the security team doing its job.

questions.explanation: SOC flagged at 11:42, encoded PowerShell to a known-bad domain, remediation approved, task removed, user confirmed she did not run anything. No action required. Happy to walk through more. All four elements present, specific, calm tone. No triumphalism.

questions.prompt: A day after your close-out, the IT manager asks: "Are these kinds of incidents going up across your customer base, or is it just us?" Best response?

choices.label: Answer with whatever you know about the broader Huntress customer base.
choices.rationale: The SOC has portfolio-level data. You do not. Speculating from a single-customer seat is the wrong source.

choices: "Good question. I don't have direct visibility into trends across other Huntress customers.

choices.label: "Yes, attacks are increasing."
choices.rationale: Plausible-sounding but unsupported. Answering from gut.

questions.explanation: I can flag this to our account manager, who can pull the Huntress quarterly trend reports for you." Honest about what you know. Useful next step. Routes to the right source.

questions.prompt: Put the four close-out elements in the order the lesson presents them.
items: What was done (named actions in plain language)
What the client should do (even if "nothing")
What was learned (when there is a real lesson)
questions.explanation: Happened, done, client action, learned. The four elements in order.

questions.prompt: When does a close-out route through a senior rather than the tech?
choices: Critical incidents, incidents with customer-relationship-sensitive findings.

choices.label: Every close-out. Close-outs are always senior work.
choices.rationale: The routine High close-out is the tech's job.

choices.label: Only when the customer asks for it in writing.
choices.rationale: Not in the lesson.

choices.label: Only when Huntress support is involved.
choices.rationale: Huntress support involvement is not the escalation criterion.

questions.explanation: Or close-outs on customers with tense relationships. Three triggers: Critical, sensitive findings, tense customer relationships.
